name: Fool Creek
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Millard County with excellent agate and jasper specimens. Limited crowds and scenic high desert terrain make this a rewarding location for collectors of all levels.
description:
Fool Creek is a public BLM rockhounding area in central Utah's Millard County, offering free access to collectors seeking quality agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. The site sits within the Basin and Range province where Tertiary-age volcanic and hydrothermal activity deposited silica-rich minerals in fractures and nodules. This remote location attracts serious rockhounds seeking solitude and uncrowded collecting opportunities.

The terrain consists of high desert scrubland at approximately 5,000 feet elevation, featuring exposed wash bottoms and hillside outcrops where specimens naturally erode from surrounding rock. Collectors will encounter colorful agate nodules ranging from translucent to opaque, often with distinctive banding patterns. Red, orange, and brown jasper pieces are common finds, particularly in the creek wash areas where water action concentrates heavier minerals.

As BLM public land, Fool Creek requires no permit and enforces a straightforward 25 lb daily limit per person. The remote setting means minimal development and few fellow collectors, though this isolation demands self-reliance and proper preparation. Spring and fall offer ideal collecting conditions when temperatures moderate and afternoon thunderstorms are less frequent.
history: This area lies within the Sevier Plateau section of the Basin and Range province, where Oligocene to Miocene volcanic activity created extensive ash-fall tuffs and silica-rich flows. Groundwater circulation through these volcanic rocks deposited chalcedony and agate nodules in cavities and fractures over millions of years, concentrating collectible specimens in dry creek washes and eroded hillsides.
tips: Search the Fool Creek wash bottom first after spring runoff when water action has freshly exposed specimens and concentrated heavier agate nodules on the streambed. Look for translucent chalcedony and banded agate half-buried in sandy-silty washes; avoid the hardest specimens unless you plan to break and polish them. Bring plenty of water (3+ liters per person), a sturdy digging tool for extracting nodules from softer matrix, and a GPS unit since landmarks are minimal in the sagebrush terrain. Check the BLM's Fillmore Field Office website before visiting to confirm current land status and access conditions.
terrain_notes: High desert scrubland at 5,000 feet elevation with gentle to moderate slopes. Sparse sagebrush and juniper scattered across rolling terrain. Main collecting areas accessible via short walks (under 1 mile) from vehicle parking. Creek wash bottom features sandy-silty footing with scattered rocks and loose material. Hillside outcrops offer harder walking on exposed volcanic rock with sparse vegetation. No developed trails; cross-country navigation required using GPS or landmarks.
best_season: October - April. Spring (March-April) and fall (October-November) offer moderate temperatures, lower afternoon thunderstorm risk, and snowmelt in the creek exposing fresh specimens. Summer heat exceeds 90F with intense UV exposure and afternoon flash flood potential in washes. Winter snowfall can close access in January-February.
written_directions: From Millard County, head south on US 89 toward Salina. Proceed approximately 15 miles south and watch for informal dirt pullouts on the east side of the highway near Fool Creek drainage markers. Turn east onto a rough dirt track (high-clearance vehicle recommended) and drive 2-3 miles to the main collecting area near the wash bottom. GPS coordinates (39.469672, -112.324314) mark the primary access point. The route is unmarked and may be rough after seasonal rains; 4x4 vehicles provide safer access.
road_conditions: Rough unpaved dirt track with ruts and washboard. High-clearance vehicle strongly recommended; standard passenger cars risk undercarriage damage. Impassable after heavy rain or snowmelt until roads dry.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and open ground near the creek wash. No developed parking area or facilities. Space available for 3-5 vehicles; park off the main track to allow passage.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Pack out all trash. Do not damage surrounding rock formations or vegetation.
rules:

nearest_services: Salina, Utah (approximately 20 miles south on US 89) offers gas, food, lodging, and emergency services. Millard County Sheriff and nearest hospital (Central Valley Medical Center in Salina) are critical resources given remote location.
accessibility_notes: Moderate difficulty terrain with uneven rocky wash bottom and scattered loose material. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Rough dirt track requires high-clearance vehicle; standard cars should not attempt access. Sturdy hiking boots required. Short walks (under 1 mile) to primary collecting zones, but hillside scrambling involves rocky footing and minor elevation changes. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility.

faq.question: What types of rocks and gemstones can you find at Fool Creek, Utah?
faq.answer: Fool Creek is known for agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. Collectors frequently find translucent to opaque banded agate nodules in shades of red, orange, white, and brown. Jasper pieces are especially common in the creek wash where water action concentrates specimens. Chalcedony nodules and fragments are also regularly discovered in the surrounding volcanic hillsides.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Fool Creek free, and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Fool Creek is completely free. As BLM public land, no permit is required for recreational collecting. You must follow the 25 lb daily limit per person and collect for personal use only. Commercial collection and selling specimens are prohibited on federal land.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Fool Creek in Millard County?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Spring (March-April) and fall (October-November) offer moderate temperatures and lower afternoon thunderstorm risk. Spring snowmelt also freshly exposes specimens in the creek wash. Summer temperatures exceed 90F with intense heat, and winter snowfall can block access. Avoid monsoon season (July-August) when flash floods threaten dry washes.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need for rockhounding at Fool Creek?
faq.answer: Bring a sturdy digging tool or small pick to extract agate nodules from softer matrix, a 5-gallon bucket or sack for specimens, a GPS unit (landmarks are minimal in sagebrush terrain), and at least 3 liters of water per person. Wear sturdy hiking boots for rough terrain and bring sun protection. A long-handled probe is useful for checking loose areas in the wash for buried nodules.

faq.question: Is Fool Creek rockhounding site suitable for beginners and families with kids?
faq.answer: Yes, Fool Creek is beginner-friendly for rockhounders aged 8 and older. The site requires no permit knowledge and agate/jasper specimens are relatively easy to spot and extract. However, the remote location (4/5 remoteness rating) and rough road access demand proper preparation, a reliable vehicle, plenty of water, and GPS navigation. Families should assess their comfort with remote self-reliance before visiting.

faq.question: How do you access Fool Creek from the nearest town, and what vehicle do you need?
faq.answer: From Salina (approximately 20 miles south), take US 89 south and locate informal pullouts near Fool Creek drainage. Turn east on a rough, unmarked dirt track and drive 2-3 miles to the main collecting area. A high-clearance vehicle (AWD or 4x4) is strongly recommended; standard passenger cars risk undercarriage damage. The route becomes impassable after heavy rain or snowmelt. Always check BLM conditions before departing.
